Long Island has some the finest beaches the world.
What can compare for beauty with Main Beach East Hampton? Malibu? Cannes? Miami? Those beaches are all thin, puny strips sand. Incredibly overrated. They could all fit any Long Island beach the same time and there would plenty shoreline spare.
never get tired looking our beautiful beach East Hampton. And when stare and look long enough, always think back what used be. When you grew in, they say, "limited circumstances," trip the beach was the best celebration the line "The best things life are free."
grew West 7th Street Brooklyn, one subway stop (five minutes) from Coney Island. The Sea Beach train (now called the ran behind our tiny house when was kid. Every time the train rolled into the station entire house would shake. night would lie bed and listen the train come in. was friendly, familiar sound little kid: meant people were coming home.
the summer Mom would take and little brother Coney Island just about every day. For me, was like traveling Oz.
When you walked off the subway, some incredible smells fought with each other get into your nose. The first was the smell raw clams being squirted with lemon. And then there was the smell ice-cold beer foaming and out the glass the clam bar that was the promenade the subway terminal.
you walked across the street, you smelled the sweetness cotton candy and two seconds later you smelled the garlic and spices those sizzling Nathan's hot dogs that made your mouth water. the time you got the boardwalk, you were starving and reaching your bag homemade sandwiches sneak bite.
You could get the beach walking onto the boardwalk under it. few years later the Drifters would tell the world about the wonders that could found "Under the Boardwalk.") Walking under was the faster way get onto the beach and that's the way you always went. You braved the cold clammy sand that hadn't felt the sunlight years. You gingerly stepped over (while still managing sneak peek at) the teenage couples who were passionately "making out" the blankets the dim semi-privacy that could only found under the boardwalk. They pretended they were invisible. was very young but knew enough along with the pretense.
The walk the beach was joke. There seemed millions people the beach; consequently, there was beach. stepped one beach blanket after another. Finally, mom staked out claim and parked our blanket, touching four other blankets, and rushed the water.
honest, the water Coney Island was just slightly cleaner than the Ganges India. The recent oil spill disaster triggered sense memory that hadn't thought about many years. For years after World War II, the water Coney Island was filled with oil chunks that blackened our feet. remember father telling that probably came from one the ships that had been blown nearby during the war. remember wondering was one ours one theirs.
But now the smell the air was suntan oil, and kid remember staying the water for hours fight the waves. Invariably, mother would call because "your lips are turning blue." She never came into the water. She just joined all the other mothers who were standing the shore "blue lip patrol." When the time came home, always begged for another half-hour. They always gave me.
the one-stop ride home, would rush the front the first car and stand toes the open window. The cool air hitting sunburned face would feel wonderful.
favorite line any movie from Atlantic City. very old Burt Lancaster trying impress very young Susan Sarandon. They're looking the Atlantic Ocean. She says, "It's very beautiful." says, "Yes." Then looks her and says, "But this nothing. You should have seen the old days."
children have the same ocean had the old days. But they don't have the noise subway trains every night. They've never been Coney Island. The beach they walk East Hampton spotlessly clean and well maintained. There's always plenty room. Thanks the diligence the village fathers, they will never smell food the way Main Beach.
children seem have everything, but some ways feel sorry for them. With all they have, they will never have the richness these memories.
